# Red Ink

> Red Ink makes the strongest, best-sourced conservative case on the numbers behind U.S. policy. Every figure is tied to a primary source (U.S. Treasury, CBO, BLS, IRS, EIA, NY Fed, Fed), and we steelman the opposing case fairly before answering it. The presenter is AI; the data is real and cited. We argue from the right; we never fudge a number.

## Explainers
- [How big is the U.S. national debt?](https://redinkpolicy.github.io/federal-debt.html): About $39.4 trillion (July 2026, U.S. Treasury) — ~$115,000 per person, growing ~$8B/day. What the number means, who we owe, why net interest now costs more than defense — and CBO's own baseline showing revenues above the 50-year average while deficits run near double it (a spending problem, not a revenue problem). Sources: Treasury, CBO (pub. 61882), JEC, CRFB, PGPF.
- [Why is inflation back in 2026?](https://redinkpolicy.github.io/inflation.html): CPI hit 4.2% in May 2026, but energy drove 60%+ of the jump and gas is up 40.5% YoY; core is 2.9%. It's an oil-supply shock, not broad inflation. Source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (CPI, May 2026).
- [Who actually pays federal income tax?](https://redinkpolicy.github.io/who-pays-income-tax.html): The top 10% pay 70.5% and the bottom 50% pay 3.3% of federal income tax; ~30% of filers owe none — with the honest caveat that payroll/sales taxes flatten the full burden. Source: IRS Statistics of Income, tax year 2023 (via Tax Foundation, NTU).
- [Why is the Fed keeping interest rates high in 2026?](https://redinkpolicy.github.io/fed-interest-rates.html): The Fed held at 3.50–3.75% in June 2026 and raised its year-end projection to 3.8% (from 3.4% in March) after inflation jumped — leaning toward hikes, not cuts. What it costs households and the federal debt. Source: Federal Reserve Summary of Economic Projections, June 17, 2026.
- [Are student loan defaults rising in 2026?](https://redinkpolicy.github.io/student-loan-defaults.html): After the pandemic pause ended, ~1M borrowers defaulted in Q4 2025 and 2.6M more in Q1 2026 (~3.6M); 17%+ have hit 90 days late; ~7M former SAVE borrowers face a second wave. SAVE was terminated: servicers began 90-day switch-or-be-switched notices to ~7M borrowers July 1, 2026; the new RAP plan (no $0 payments, 30-year term, interest waiver) opened the same day. Sources: NY Fed (Liberty Street Economics, May 2026); U.S. Dept. of Education; Federal Student Aid.
- [Is nuclear power safe?](https://redinkpolicy.github.io/nuclear-safety.html): By deaths per TWh, nuclear (~0.03) is the second-safest source and ~800x safer than coal (~24.6), even counting Chernobyl and Fukushima; the real obstacle is build cost/time, not safety. Source: Our World in Data.
- [Is a college degree still worth it?](https://redinkpolicy.github.io/is-college-worth-it.html): A bachelor's earns ~$2.8M lifetime vs ~$1.6M for high school (~$1.2M / 75% more) — but the average hides huge variance; major and occupation often matter more than the diploma. Source: Georgetown CEW, "The College Payoff."
- [Why does nuclear power cost so much to build in America?](https://redinkpolicy.github.io/why-nuclear-costs-so-much.html): Vogtle's two reactors ran from a ~$14B estimate to ~$36.8B and ~7 years late; V.C. Summer spent ~$9B for zero reactors. The cost is self-inflicted (regulation, one-off designs, a decayed supply chain), not physics or safety — Korea built 4 reactors at Barakah (~5,600 MW) for ~$24–32B. The June 23, 2026 DOE $17.5B conditional loan targets the supply-chain/time bottleneck for up to 10 AP1000 reactors. Sources: DOE, World Nuclear News, ANS, Utility Dive, Our World in Data.
- [How much does the U.S. pay in interest on the national debt?](https://redinkpolicy.github.io/debt-interest-costs.html): About $1 trillion a year. Net interest hit $970B in FY2025 — more than defense or Medicare, second only to Social Security — and ran $723B through May of FY2026 (+~9%). CBO projects $1.0T (2026) rising to $2.1T by 2036. Sources: U.S. Treasury Monthly Treasury Statement, CBO.
- [Do zoning rules really make housing expensive?](https://redinkpolicy.github.io/do-zoning-laws-make-housing-expensive.html): Rent is up ~36% since May 2019 (BLS CPI) and the median new home costs ~$403,000 (Census/HUD). Los Angeles was zoned for 10M people in 1960, only 4.3M by 2016 — legal supply caps, not physics, drive the shortage. The fix is legalizing building, not subsidizing scarcity. Sources: Census/HUD & BLS via FRED, White House Housing Development Toolkit, Hsieh & Moretti (AEJ 2019).
- [Is the U.S. job market actually strong right now?](https://redinkpolicy.github.io/is-the-job-market-actually-strong.html): No. June 2026 added just 57,000 jobs (12-month average: 36,000/month) with Apr/May revised down a combined 74,000. Unemployment "fell" to 4.2% because participation dropped to 61.5% — the lowest since March 2021 — and wages (+3.5%) trail prices (+4.2%): real pay is falling. 47K of June's gains came from government-funded health care/social assistance while leisure & hospitality cut 61K. Source: BLS Employment Situation, June 2026.
- [What is the new student loan Repayment Assistance Plan (RAP)?](https://redinkpolicy.github.io/what-is-the-repayment-assistance-plan-rap.html): The plan that went live July 1, 2026 (P.L. 119-21): payments 1–10% of AGI with a $10/month minimum, unpaid interest waived plus up to $50/month matched toward principal for on-time payers, forgiveness at 30 years. Sole plan for new loans; existing borrowers choose by July 2028. Fair caveat: AGI-based payments can cost low earners more than SAVE did. Sources: CRS IF13075, U.S. Dept. of Education, NY Fed.

## News
- [The Receipt Feed](https://redinkpolicy.github.io/news.html): Red Ink's curated weekly feed of policy/economic news, each story paired with the primary-source number behind it and a one-line take. Right-of-center lens, sourced. Updated weekly.

## About
- [All links / link hub](https://redinkpolicy.github.io): Every Red Ink platform.
- [The Receipt — free newsletter](https://redinkpolicy.github.io/newsletter.html): Plain-English policy explainers, twice a week, free.

## Sourcing standard
Every numeric claim on a Red Ink page links to the primary government or nonpartisan source for that figure. Pages show a visible published and last-updated date. If a claim can't be sourced, it isn't published.
